title: DMT and Kundalini: The Overlap Between Endogenous and Exogenous Experiences

body:
DMT and Kundalini: The Overlap Between Endogenous and Exogenous Experiences

You're here because you're scared. I get it. You've either blasted off on DMT and felt something… else. Something beyond a trip. Or you're dealing with kundalini, and it feels like your brain's on fire and your body's a live wire. Maybe you've done both, and the lines are blurring. You're wondering if you're losing your mind, if you've broken something inside yourself that can't be fixed. I've been there. That terrified, alone-at-3am feeling? Yeah, I know it well. I've walked through that fire, and I'm still here. So, let's talk, because you're not crazy. You're just experiencing something most people can't even begin to imagine.

You're probably feeling isolated, Right? Like no one truly understands the sheer intensity of what's happening. The visions, the sounds, the bodily sensations that are so real they're more real than reality itself. The fear that you've opened a door that can't be closed. And if you're dealing with kundalini, it's not a choice, is it? It just happened. And now you're trying to make sense of the chaos.

I'll tell you what - the connection between DMT and kundalini isn't just some fringe theory. There's a deep, unsettling, and incredibly significant overlap. One is often seen as an external trigger, a chemical key. The other is an internal process, an awakening that can feel like an invasion. But they both take you to strikingly similar places. They both blow your perception of reality to smithereens, leaving you to pick up the pieces and try to figure out what's real and what isn't. And that's terrifying, believe me, I know.

The DMT Experience: A Glimpse Behind the Veil

So, you've tried DMT. Or you're thinking about it. You've heard the stories: meeting entities, dissolving into light, experiencing other dimensions. It's often called "the spirit molecule" for a reason. It's fast, it's intense, and it doesn't mess around. One minute you're here, the next you're somewhere completely alien, yet strangely familiar. It's like your brain suddenly remembers things it shouldn't know. Like it's accessing a library of universal information, or maybe just experiencing a massive download of raw consciousness data.

What's happening? Well, dimethyltryptamine is a powerful psychedelic. It's found in plants, animals, and - get this - your own brain. Yeah, your brain makes it. That's a huge part of why this conversation is so important. Some theories suggest it's released during birth, death, dreams, or even during intense spiritual experiences. So, when you smoke it, or vape it, or inject it, you're basically flooding your system with a chemical that your body already knows how to make, but in quantities that usually only happen at life's most extreme thresholds.

The experience itself? It's often described as a breakthrough. You go through a "waiting room," a tunnel, or a fractal kaleidoscope, and then you're there. There's often a feeling of immense love, or sometimes immense terror. You might encounter what people call "machine elves" or other beings. You might see patterns, colors, and geometries that defy description. You might feel like you're being operated on, initiated, or simply observed. Time ceases to exist. Your sense of self dissolves. You're just pure awareness, floating in an ocean of information.

And then it's over. Usually in 10-20 minutes. But those minutes? They can feel like an eternity. And the after-effects? They can linger for days, weeks, even months. You see the world differently. You question everything. You might feel a deep connection to something larger than yourself, or you might be left with a deep sense of disorientation, wondering what the hell just happened and if anything you thought was real actually is. You know what I mean? It's not a casual drug experience for most people. It's a seismic shift.

Kundalini: The Internal Inferno

Now, kundalini. This is where it gets really personal for me. I didn't take a substance for this. It just... activated. It's often described as a coiled serpent energy at the base of the spine. In spiritual traditions, it's meant to awaken through years of disciplined yoga, meditation, and spiritual practice. But for some of us, it just switches on, often without warning, without preparation, and without a damn instruction manual.

It's not gentle. It's not peaceful, not at first. It's a torrent. It's fire. It's electricity. It's like every nerve ending in your body is suddenly plugged into a high-voltage current. You get intense heat, vibrations, involuntary muscle spasms, tremors, and sometimes a feeling like something is literally crawling up your spine. You might hear internal sounds - buzzing, roaring, flutes, bells. You might see internal lights, geometric patterns, or even visions of deities or other beings. You might feel intense pressure in your head, like your skull is expanding or being squeezed.

And the psychological stuff? Oh god, the psychological stuff. Intense anxiety, panic attacks, depression that comes out of nowhere, feelings of depersonalization and derealization. You might experience spontaneous memories, past-life regressions, or a sudden, overwhelming urge to change everything about your life. Your senses might become hypersensitive - sounds are too loud, lights are too bright, smells are too strong. You feel raw, exposed, and incredibly vulnerable. You feel like you're losing control, because you are. Your ego is being dismantled, whether you like it or not. I've lived through this. It's not a metaphor. It's a very real, very physical, very terrifying process.

People will tell you it's psychosis. Doctors will try to medicate you. Friends and family will look at you like you've gone mad. And you'll start to believe them, because who could possibly explain what's happening? But here's the truth: it's an awakening. A forced, brutal awakening, but an awakening nonetheless. It's your consciousness expanding, your energetic system being rewired. And it's not just some woo-woo concept. There are actual physical and neurological changes happening in your body and brain. You can read about it in books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). These aren't just spiritual texts; they're accounts of real people going through this. They're not going to fix it for you, but they'll make you feel a little less alone.

The Eerie Overlap: Same Destination, Different Paths

So, you've got DMT, an external chemical that triggers an intense, often entity-laden, multidimensional experience. And you've got kundalini, an internal energetic process that also leads to intense bodily sensations, altered states of consciousness, visions, and encounters with seemingly non-physical entities. See the connection? It's not a coincidence. The subjective reports from both experiences often mirror each other with frightening accuracy.

  • Altered Perception of Reality: Both experiences shatter your normal sense of what's real. The world suddenly seems permeable, layered, and far more complex than you ever imagined.
  • Encounters with Entities: Whether it's the "machine elves" of DMT or the internal visions of deities, guides, or shadowy figures in kundalini, the feeling of interacting with non-physical beings is a common thread. They can feel benevolent, neutral, or even hostile.
  • Body Sensations: The intense vibrations, heat, tingling, and electrical sensations reported in kundalini are often present in the come-up or come-down of a DMT trip. It's like your nervous system is being recalibrated, or overloaded.
  • Dissolution of Ego: Both experiences can completely obliterate your sense of self. Your personal history, your identity, your ego - it all melts away, leaving you as pure, undifferentiated consciousness. This can be incredibly liberating or utterly terrifying, depending on your state of mind.
  • Access to Information: Many report gaining insights, knowledge, or understanding that feels ancient and universal. It's like tapping into a collective unconscious or a cosmic database.
  • Time Distortion: Minutes feel like hours, or hours feel like seconds. Linear time breaks down, and you might experience past, present, and future simultaneously.
  • Geometric Patterns and Light: Both paths often involve seeing incredibly complex, moving geometric patterns, fractals, and intense, multi-colored light.

Here's the thing: if your brain produces DMT, and DMT can trigger these states, then it stands to reason that intense internal processes like kundalini activation might involve the endogenous release of such powerful neurochemicals. It's not a stretch to think that your own body, under extreme energetic conditions, can produce its own "psychedelic" experience. That's why the sensations and visions are so similar. Your brain isn't just seeing things; it's being flooded with the very chemicals that allow it to perceive these "other" realities.

So yeah, you're not imagining it. The feeling that these experiences are related is valid. You're not crazy for seeing the parallels. You're just noticing something that science is only beginning to scratch the surface of. It's a deep, deep rabbit hole, and you're in it. And it's okay to be scared. It's a natural reaction to having your entire worldview dismantled.

Managing the Crisis: When Normal Life Isn't an Option

Look, whether it's the after-effects of a powerful DMT journey or the ongoing intensity of kundalini, you're in a crisis. This isn't something you just "get over." It's a deep, life-altering event. And you need to treat it that way. You can't just go back to your old life and pretend nothing happened. It won't work. Your nervous system is fried, your perception is altered, and your sense of self is probably shattered.

Practical Steps for Survival

  • Ground Yourself: This is crucial. You're probably floating, disassociated, or overwhelmed. Get back into your body. Go for walks in nature, feel the earth under your feet. Eat grounding foods - root vegetables, heavy grains. Take warm baths. Focus on your breath. Anything that brings you back to the physical present moment.
  • Limit Stimulation: Your senses are probably overloaded. Turn off the news, stay away from loud environments, limit screen time. Give your nervous system a break.
  • Routine, Routine, Routine: Even if you feel like you're spiraling, try to establish some kind of daily rhythm. Wake up at the same time, eat at the same time, go to bed at the same time. This creates a sense of stability when everything else feels chaotic.
  • Find a Safe Space: If you can, retreat from obligations and responsibilities. You need time and space to process. If you have understanding friends or family, lean on them. If not, it's okay to isolate a bit to protect your energy.
  • Journal: Write down everything you're experiencing. The visions, the sensations, the thoughts, the fears. It helps externalize it and can give you a sense of control over the overwhelming flood of information.
  • Seek Understanding, Not Just Treatment: Most doctors won't get it. They'll diagnose you with anxiety, psychosis, or depression and push meds. While medication can sometimes be a temporary crutch to get you through the worst of it, it often doesn't address the root of what's happening. You need someone who understands spiritual emergence, not just mental illness. Books like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) and Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) were lifesavers for me. They show you that you're not alone in this kind of experience.
  • Breathwork and Gentle Movement: If you can, try very gentle yoga or tai chi. Don't push yourself. The idea isn't to intensify the energy, but to help it move and integrate. Breathwork can help regulate your nervous system.
  • Diet: Eat clean, nourishing foods. Avoid processed sugars, caffeine, and alcohol. Your body is under immense stress; give it the best fuel you can.

This isn't an overnight fix. It's a long haul. It's a journey through hell, honestly. But you're not going to be stuck here forever. The intensity will eventually subside. You'll learn to integrate these experiences. You'll rebuild yourself, but you'll be different. You'll be changed. You'll have a deeper understanding of reality, of consciousness, and of your own incredible resilience. Does that land? Because it's true.

Integration: The Long Road Back (or Forward)

The hardest part isn't the experience itself; it's what comes after. It's trying to fit a universe-sized experience into your human life. It's trying to explain the unexplainable. It's trying to function when your operating system has been completely rewritten. But here's the thing: you can do it. You have to. Because once you've seen behind the veil, you can't unsee it.

Integration means making sense of what happened. It means finding new ways to live with your expanded awareness. It means letting go of the old you, because that person is gone. You're not going "back to normal." You're moving forward into a new normal. And it's going to be messy. You're going to have doubts. You're going to have moments of deep clarity and moments of utter confusion. That's all part of the process.

I won't lie to you; it's incredibly hard. You'll face skepticism, misunderstanding, and loneliness. But you'll also find strength you didn't know you had. You'll develop a deeper connection to yourself, to others, and to the universe. You'll learn what truly matters. And you'll come out the other side, not unscathed, but transformed. You're going through a spiritual emergency, and it's okay to acknowledge that. It's a real thing, and you're not alone. Hang in there. You're stronger than you think.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
